content.problemStatement:
This problem has the same setup as `KUPSET`. The only difference is that here, you only need to minimize the number of upsets in the tournament.

You are given two integers NNN and WWW.

Construct any array AAA of length 2N+1−12^{N+1}-12N+1−1 satisfying the following properties:


A1=WA_1 = WA1​=W.
For each 1≤i<2N1 \le i \lt 2^N1≤i<2N, either Ai=max⁡(A2i,A2i+1)A_i = \max(A_{2i}, A_{2i+1})Ai​=max(A2i​,A2i+1​) or Ai=min⁡(A2i,A2i+1)A_i = \min(A_{2i}, A_{2i+1})Ai​=min(A2i​,A2i+1​).
The number of indices among these for which Ai=max⁡(A2i,A2i+1)A_i = \max(A_{2i}, A_{2i+1})Ai​=max(A2i​,A2i+1​) is **minimized**.
The last 2N2^N2N elements of AAA form a permutation of {1,2,…,2N}\{1, 2, \ldots, 2^N\}{1,2,…,2N}.
Less formally, you are to set up a single-elimination tournament bracket on players labeled {1,2,…,2N}\{1, 2, \ldots, 2^N\}{1,2,…,2N} such that the eventual winner of the tournament is WWW, and the number of upsets across the entire tournament is minimized; where an upset is defined to be a player with larger label defeating a player with smaller label.

If there are multiple possible solutions, you may find any of them.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of a single line of input, containing two space-separated integers NNN and WWW.


Output Format
For each test case, output 2N+1−12^{N+1}-12N+1−1 space-separated integers denoting the array you found.


If there are multiple possible solutions, you may find any of them.
constraints: The sum of 2N2^N2N over all test cases won't exceed 2172^{17}217.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: We have A=[1,1,3,1,2,3,4]A = [1, 1, 3, 1, 2, 3, 4]A=[1,1,3,1,2,3,4].
There are no upsets at all, and the winner is W=1W = 1W=1. This is clearly optimal.
Test case 222: We have A=[2,2,3,1,2,3,4]A = [2, 2, 3, 1, 2, 3, 4]A=[2,2,3,1,2,3,4].  Here,

A1=min⁡(A2,A3)=2A_1 = \min(A_2, A_3) = 2A1​=min(A2​,A3​)=2, so the winner of the 'final' is 222, as desired.
A2=max⁡(A4,A5)A_2 = \max(A_4, A_5)A2​=max(A4​,A5​).
A3=min⁡(A6,A7)A_3 = \min(A_6, A_7)A3​=min(A6​,A7​).

The only upset is in the match between 111 and 222, which is won by 222.
It can be proved that this is optimal: that is, it's impossible to make 222 win with less than one upset.
